data.title: Sibanye Stillwater Is More Than A Gold Miner, And I'm Long Because Of It
data.description:
Thesis

Sibanye Stillwater Limited (NYSE: NYSE:SBSW) used to be mainly a South African gold‑mining outfit, but it has turned itself into a global company that also produces lots of platinum‑group metals and is moving into battery minerals like lithium. It now runs mines and processing plants in South Africa, the United States, and Europe, and that spread makes it a player in the shift toward cleaner energy. My point is that this makeover, built around rich U.S. platinum deposits, in‑house smelting and refining, and newly revived gold and lithium projects, should let Sibanye make much bigger profits as platinum prices rise above its production costs and as U.S. tax credits boost its margins even more. Because the company has slimmed down and focuses on metals that are in tight supply, the recent jump in platinum prices is proof that the new strategy is working and that investors haven’t fully caught on to how much cash the business can now generate.

Sibanye Stillwater Turns Platinum Margins Positive in the U.S.

I get the sense that people used to think of Sibanye Stillwater mainly as a South African gold miner, though the firm likes to remind everyone that it also owns mines in other countries and produces several metals. The problem was platinum: for about two years the market price of platinum stayed below what it cost Sibanye to dig it up, so the company’s U.S. platinum mines looked like money‑losers even though they sit on great ore. Now platinum has climbed to roughly $1,453 an ounce and that new price is finally high enough for Sibanye to sell platinum for more than it costs to produce, giving the company a profit cushion. The margin isn’t huge in dollar terms, but I'd argue, compared with the recent past it’s a big, game‑changing improvement.

The recent breakout in platinum prices helps, but that’s not the main reason the company’s future looks brighter. What really matters is that Sibanye’s bosses finally tightened up how they run their U.S. mines. They put the old Stillwater West mine on temporary suspension last year, open since 1986, because it was no longer worth the trouble, showing they’ll put profits ahead of nostalgia. Instead, they’re pouring their efforts into two other Montana mines: Stillwater East, a newer, richer deposit that’s been built out since 2017, and East Boulder, which has been producing metal since 2002. These two sites churn out higher‑quality ore and cost less per ounce to run. The underground rock itself hasn’t changed, it still holds about 19 million ounces of confirmed precious metals and another 79 million ounces that are likely there, enough to keep mining for decades. What’s new, and crucial, is that the company is willing to stop digging any tonnage that loses money.

That move looks even smarter once you factor in government tax breaks. Thanks to the Inflation Reduction Act, the company will get about $90 million in tax credits for 2024 and another $30 million for 2025 because it produces key minerals inside the United States. Those credits push its total cost to mine and process platinum down to roughly $1,320–$1,360 per ounce. With platinum selling for around $1,453 an ounce, Sibanye now makes about $86 profit on every ounce it pulls out of the ground, about a 6% margin. Since its U.S. mines produced 425,842 ounces last year, that works out to more than $36 million in extra operating profit, even before any future efficiency gains or favorable exchange‑rate moves. The workforce is lean, just 911 people, so any small boost in productivity will add quickly to earnings. Plus, because the company processes and refines all that ore at its own smelter in Columbus, Ohio, the money stays in the U.S., something Washington, especially President Trump, likes as it tries to shore up domestic supply chains.

Yet, while the North American mines have swung from liability to asset, Sibanye refuses to let any single commodity dictate its fate. It has a gold project in South Africa called Burnstone that it put on hold during the downturn in platinum‑group metals, even after spending $280 million to develop it and planning for it to run 20 years at roughly 120,000 ounces a year. Management says they paused the project not because the gold is low‑quality or too expensive to mine, but because it seemed smarter to save cash when markets were roiling. Now that the company’s finances are stronger, they feel comfortable restarting Burnstone, which shows they’ve raised the bar for what counts as a worthwhile investment. Money isn’t as cheap or easy to get as it used to be, so any new gold mine has to be very attractive, and Burnstone finally meets that higher standard. Helping the case is the recent surge in gold prices: as of July 17, 2025, spot gold has climbed to around $3,334 per ounce, up more than 26% year-over-year, giving Sibanye fresh confidence that reviving the project will pay off.

Sibanye Stillwater’s Gold Revival Shifts the Earnings Power Base

I have to say that Sibanye’s growing optimism makes sense. In the last half of 2024, the company’s long‑running South African gold mines made about R 3.6 billion (rand), roughly $206 million, in operating profit, up 216% even though they produced 8% less gold. Naturally, most of that jump came from higher prices: the average selling price at the time climbed 26% to $2,560 an ounce, and, as we all know, mid‑2025 prices are staying very strong. All things considered, for the first time since 2017, South African gold became the company’s biggest moneymaker, passing its South African platinum‑group‑metal (PGM) division. That’s important because the U.S. side of the business still relies heavily on palladium, a metal hurt by oversupply and the move away from gas‑powered cars. So the stronger gold business, along with this rise in platinum, not only boosts overall income but also helps protect the company if demand for palladium keeps sliding.

Sibanye doesn’t rely only on digging new metal out of the ground. The company also scoops up about $32 million by stripping metals from junked catalytic converters in the U.S., and it pulls in another $34 million by wringing value out of old mine waste in Australia. True, those figures seem small compared with its big mines in Montana or South Africa, but these side businesses cost very little to run and score well on environmental goals. Because of that, investors who worry about the downsides of traditional mining like seeing profits come from what would otherwise be trash. Plus, I suspect the steady cash they generate helps smooth out the company’s overall earnings and protects it whenever a mine faces geological problems or political trouble.

Stronger cash generation has, predictably, beefed up the balance sheet. By the end of 2024 Sibanye was sitting on R 45.7 billion, about $2.4 billion, in spare liquidity, easily enough to kill off the $675 million bond coming due in 2026. Adjusted EBITDA for the year hit $715 million, the third straight half‑year of solid, if unspectacular, returns. The 2023‑24 belt‑tightening, lower U.S. capex and trimmed overhead, finally showed up as real free cash flow, just as rivals struggle with rising costs.

I’m willing to bet that the most emblematic transaction was a $500 million streaming deal with Franco‑Nevada in December 2024. Sibanye traded away less than 2% of its future gold and platinum output. That was for cash up front. And in one stroke, cut its net leverage from about 1.3x to roughly 0.7x. The swap did three things: it raised money without diluting shareholders, it left almost all of the upside if metal prices jump, and it gave Sibanye the freedom to buy back stock in a slump or pour cash into Finnish lithium and tailings projects if things heat up.

SBSW’s Operating Pressures, Forward Growth Outlook, and Equity Valuation

By the end of last year, Sibanye’s biggest headache was its U.S. platinum‑and‑palladium division. Palladium prices had stayed weak for so long that sales and asset values took a big hit. Yes, platinum prices perked up late in the year, but, at the time, that didn’t help much: on average, the metals mix Sibanye sold from its U.S. mines went for just $988 an ounce, down 21%. That was often less than it cost to produce, so profit margins were squeezed hard and the U.S. unit flipped from moneymaker to money loser. Because of all this, Sibanye’s operating profit (EBITDA) fell from $1.1 billion in 2023 to $715 million in 2024, a 37% drop, and after taxes and special charges the company ended up with a $311 million net loss.

Even though metal prices were swinging around, company leaders realized they had bigger long‑term problems to fix right away. So, as I pointed out, they shut down the Stillwater West mine, let go of 636 employees, and shifted their focus to two other Montana mines that produce higher‑quality ore. I’m inclined to think that move should improve overall grades, but it also means the company will haul out about 200,000 fewer ounces of metal in 2025.

Granted, Sibanye just wrote off $479 million on its U.S. platinum‑and‑palladium assets. I get the sense that the company's admitting its old forecasts for palladium demand no longer work now that hybrids and electric cars are taking over. But the company’s problems aren’t only in the United States or only about palladium. In 2024, total sales stayed basically flat at about $6.1 billion because weak prices hit both its South African and U.S. mines. South Africa’s mix of four precious metals sold for 16% less, and the U.S. mix fell 21%, as mentioned earlier. I gather that having mines in two countries and selling different metals helped a little, but not enough to offset the broad slide in prices.

On the tax front, things went from good to bad. In 2023 Sibanye actually got a $131 million tax credit, but in 2024 it had to pay $81 million in taxes. That swing squeezed the company’s cash even more. Because of that, the board decided not to pay a year‑end dividend. Giving up the dividend props up the balance sheet, sure, but it ticks off investors who expect mining stocks to spit out cash in good times. Looks to me like management would rather patch the ship and keep their options open than toss money overboard right now. That makes sense, since U.S. operations have very little wiggle room on costs and the South African mines still depend on an unreliable power grid and a volatile local currency.

Nevertheless, while the stock is up +160% YTD, I actually suspect there's more room to go higher.

At the time of my analysis, at $8.59, SBSW is 27% below what some models project it’s really worth ($11.79) and almost two‑thirds under an implied future value of $25.40.

Although the forward P/E for 2025 looks high at 16.74, it drops to 8.93 in 2026, implying earnings could double within 18 months. That upbeat view assumes Sibanye cuts costs, focuses on higher‑quality ore even if it mines less of it, and benefits from stronger platinum and gold prices, all of which would send more money straight to the bottom line.

Other valuation yardsticks tell the same story. If you compare Sibanye’s total value to its expected earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EV/EBITDA), the stock trades at about 5.7x, roughly one‑third cheaper than the industry average. Look at earnings after depreciation and amortization (EV/EBIT) and it’s about 8.1x, or a 40% discount to similar companies. The price‑to‑sales ratio is just 0.93, showing the market is skeptical.

Yet the company’s operating cash flow is projected to jump roughly 48%, about six times faster than the sector.

Because of this, a forward price‑to‑cash‑flow of 3.62 hints at real upside if those cash flows appear.

Final Verdict

Rating: Buy. I think the shares still trade at a steep discount to fair value even after a big year‑to‑date run, and the combination of rising platinum and gold prices, leaner U.S. operations, and a stronger balance sheet should let earnings rebound sharply. Granted, costs remain tight and palladium demand is a headwind, but the tax credits, streaming cash, and higher‑grade focus give the company enough cushion that I’m comfortable calling this a buy rather than just a hold.

data.title: Lennox Q2 Preview: Price Increase And Volume Decline Ahead, Initiate With Hold
data.description:
Lennox International Inc. (NYSE:LII) manufactures Furnaces, air conditioners, heat pumps and refrigeration systems, operating through Home Comfort Solutions and Building Climate Solutions segments in both residential and commercial markets. Lennox planned to adjust its product price effective early in Q2 FY25 to mitigate its tariff costs. I think the company is well positioned to capture the HVAC market growth; however, I think the stock price is slightly overvalued; therefore, I am initiating with a Hold rating with a fair value of $573 per share.

As detailed in the table below, Lennox has experienced robust growth in both topline and bottom line in recent years, driven by strong pricing growth in both Home Comfort Solutions and Building Climate Solutions segments. In addition, the management has done a great job to manage its overhead costs, expanding its overall net income margins with 26.2% year-over-year growth in FY24.

As depicted in the slide below, Lennox has worked on its factory automation and distribution profitability initiatives to reduce costs and accelerate its business growth over the past few years. As noted in its call, 90% of its cost structures are based in the North America, which has minimized its risks to the U.S.-China trade disputes and tariffs. Notably, only 5% of their business is exposed to the impact from U.S.-China tariff duties.

However, due to the tariffs on aluminum and steel, Lennox anticipates a 9% cost inflation for FY25, as noted in its recent earnings call. The management announced the price increases in early Q2 to mitigate the tariff duties, anticipating a 7% price increase, up from previous 1% guidance. While I believe the overall HVAC market will raise price because of tariffs, it is likely that Lennox cannot fully offset its cost pressure in the near term.

Lennox released its Q1 FY25 result on April 23, delivering 2% organic revenue growth with 4% decline in volume, 1% growth from price and 5% increase from product mix, as detailed in the chart below:

Due to the tariff costs and tariff impacts and factory inefficiencies, its segment profits declined by 7% during the quarter. During the earnings call, the management noted that the new factories for Building Climate Solutions business experienced production inefficiencies due to ramp-up and regulatory transitions.

My key takeaway from this quarter was the 9% volume decline in its commercial business caused by weak demands and channel destocking activities. The management anticipated half of the volume decline was due to destocking and end-customers pre-purchases in anticipation of upcoming tariffs. It is likely that the volume pressure from pre-buy activities will remain in the upcoming quarter.

Lennox is scheduled to release its Q2 FY25 result on July 23 before the market open. The company is guiding for 2% revenue growth and $22.25 to $23.50 in EPS for the full year, as detailed in the slide below:

Due to the pre-buy activities in FY24, the company anticipates the total volume will decline by 4% for FY25. Indeed, Lennox is facing substantial cost inflation coupled with volume pressure for this fiscal year.

As the Trump’s administration recently announced a 50% tariff on copper effective August 1, I think the management might adjust its cost inflation estimates in the upcoming quarter. The rising raw material costs will become a significant headwind for the company’s margin expansion for FY25, in my opinion.

For FY25, I anticipate Lennox will deliver 2% revenue growth, in line with its guidance. I project its operating margin will decline by 20bps in FY25, assuming its price adjustment in Q2 cannot fully offset the tariff costs on steel, aluminum and copper. From FY26 onwards, I project the revenue will normalize to 7% of growth assuming 3% pricing growth and 4% volume growth, which aligns with the average growth rate over the past six years. I project 20bps annual margin expansion from FY26 onwards, driven by 10bps from pricing increases and 10bps from reduction in SG&A costs. I calculate the normalized operating expenses will grow by 6.8% annually, leading to a 19.9% operating margin by FY34.

It's worth noting that Lennox maintains a robust balance sheet with a net debt leverage of 0.8x. In May 2025, the company announced a $1 billion increase to its existing stock repurchase authorization. As a result, I anticipate the total number of shares outstanding will decline by 1% annually.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate FCFF as follows:

I estimate the WACC to be 9.9% assuming risk free 4.2%; beta 1.01; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $21 billion; debt $1.1 billion; tax rate 20%. I set the terminal growth to be 5% assuming 3% price growth and 2% volume growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $573 per share, according to my DCF.

Key Risks

While Lennox has limited tariff exposure in China, the company has huge exposures to U.S.-Mexico tariff risks. As shown in the table below, Mexico accounts for more than 30% of total property, plant and equipment. If the U.S. government imposes any tariffs to Mexico, Lennox will face additional cost pressures on top of the sector tariffs on steel, aluminum and copper.

While around 75% of Lennox’s businesses are tied to the replacement market, a quarter of its revenues are tied to the new construction market, which is very sensitive to the interest rate and macro environment. The U.S. housing market has experienced sluggish growth in recent months due to high mortgage rates, and it is unlikely for the company to generate meaningful volume growth from its new construction market.

Lastly, more than 30% of its employees are represented by unions, according to its 10K. The union structures will limit the company’s capabilities to structure its workforce and reallocate its manufacturing facilities in the future.

Conclusion

While Lennox International Inc. faces near-term challenges in volume growth, destocking activities and cost inflations, I think the company has the pricing power to gradually pass these additional costs to its end-customers over time. I am initiating with a Hold rating with a fair value of $573 per LII share.

data.title: FDIS: Consumer Discretionary Dashboard For July
data.description:
This article reports industry metrics, aiming at a top-down analysis of the consumer discretionary sector. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) and Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FDIS), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

data.title: Standard Chartered: Taking Stock After The Big 2025 Rally
data.description:
The market has come around to Standard Chartered PLC (OTCPK:SCBFF), a UK-domiciled regional banking group that derives most of its profits from Asia, since my prior bullish calls (see Standard Chartered: Stay The Course and Standard Chartered: High-Quality Global Bank Priced At A Deep Discount). So much so that the group, despite its outsized exposure to tariffs (via the trade finance and Asia-centric nature of its business), has been among the year’s best-performing banking names.

Coming off a solid Q1 and some very positive signals into its upcoming Q2 report as well, Standard Chartered looks to be full steam ahead. That said, there have also been some significant new developments recently, Namely, a $2.7bn lawsuit relating to a Malaysian investment fund scandal, as well as interest rate shifts and flows relating to its wealth management unit, all of which warrant a closer look.

On the $2.7bn 1MDB Bombshell

Standard Chartered was rocked with a surprise civil lawsuit earlier this month. The allegation, by the liquidators of Malaysian state investment fund 1MDB, relates to compliance failures that allowed over $2.7bn to be laundered through the bank during the 2009-2013 period. In response, Standard Chartered stated that they “consider these claims are without merit” and “will vigorously defend against the claims”. The group’s press release added that they “reported the transaction activities of these companies” and “shut their accounts in early 2013”.

At first glance, a $2.7bn liability seems significant enough to warrant investor attention. But there are important caveats here. For one, even if successful, litigation will drag on for a while, so any one-off fine is likely to be stretched over many years. Secondly, the 1MDB issue isn’t new – Standard Chartered has already come under regulatory scrutiny from the Monetary Authority of Singapore (Singapore’s financial regulator or “MAS”) back in 2016, with the investigation finding no “pervasive control weaknesses or wilful misconduct”.

Now, even in a worst-case scenario, where Standard Chartered does incur the full $2.7bn fine, it’s worth noting that the group has more than enough cushion to absorb this hit. For context, the group reported tier 1 capital of $42.6bn in Q1 2025, of which common equity tier-1 capital (i.e., CET1 or the highest quality capital immediately available for loss absorption) contributed $35.1bn.

This capital base is only going to grow in the coming years as well, helped by $4-$5bn of yearly profits, so I see no liquidity or solvency risk at this point. As for Standard Chartered’s $8bn distribution target, the ample capital buffer at hand means its current +10% total shareholder return yield (dividends + buybacks) is likely to be unaffected as well.

On Rates and Cross-Border Flows

Declining interest rates, as we are seeing throughout Asia, are a big negative for bank margins, and Standard Chartered is no different. Note, for instance, the continued decline in net interest income in recent quarters. And with tariffs knocking a few points off Asian growth as well, Q1 2025 estimates saw the group’s currency-weighted average rate assumption further declining by a cumulative ~141bps through fiscal year 2026.

In Standard Chartered’s case, however, there are unique mitigating factors. For one, while the previously published sensitivity at the H1 2024 interim outlined a $650m per 100bp rate move, today’s rate sensitivity is likely a lot lower thanks to “materially increased” hedging in recent quarters.

The group’s leadership in an increasingly active Hong Kong-China corridor (largely thanks to record-low interest rates in mainland China) has also been useful in attracting deposits out of China and cushioning the margin impact. From here, all signs point to Chinese policy rates falling further as growth slows and tariffs kick in; in this scenario, Standard Chartered is well placed to benefit.

On the Wealth Management Opportunity

As resilient as the group has proven to be through near-term tariff headwinds (recall management citing a “positive start” to Q2 on the last earnings call), the key reason to own Standard Chartered remains its longer-term wealth management opportunity. For context, the goal here, centered on serving a growing affluent class in Asia, is $200bn of net new money and double-digit “wealth solutions” annualized growth through 2029.

So far, the group, having heavily ramped up hiring in recent years, is making good on its promises. Mainly by onboarding new-to-bank affluent customers, but also by up-tiering existing clientele, all of whom are ‘sticky’; as management describes, these are “very long-term savers” and “very long-term investors” that come with low retention costs. As things stand, Standard Chartered is seeing a very significant acceleration in net new affluent money (+22% YoY) from its shift, driving a $389bn base of affluent assets in Q1.

Heading into Q2 reporting later this month, it’s encouraging that the group hasn’t seen any adverse impact from “Liberation Day” in April. Per management, things have actually been positive, as “by all the metrics into the early part of Q2, we've seen new clients, new money and income performing with the momentum that we saw in Q1.” The key here is Standard Chartered’s one-stop shop offering, which includes more defensive products and should thus continue to mitigate outflows if we do see more macro uncertainty going forward.

Summing Up

In all, with Standard Chartered already tracking at the upper end of its mid-term targets and sustaining that momentum into early 2025, the setup looks good into earnings later this month. In the meantime, valuations, while higher following this year’s big rally, still leave room for a lot more upside at the current ~20% discount to forward book. From here, look for further progress on return on capital (over 14% return on tangible equity target) and return of capital (over 10% total distribution yield via buybacks + dividends) to catalyze the next leg higher.

data.title: TSMC Q2: AI Growth Is Booming, Yet I'm Holding Off
data.description:
Since my last Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) analysis, the stock gas gained 7% in price.

TSMC just posted Q2 results, sending the stock toward record highs. I personally deem the stock a little overvalued right now, based on its historical sentiment and the maturity of the AI capex cycle. However, many momentum and growth investors see TSMC stock more positively, given a robust future growth outlook and record Q2 earnings showing strong operating efficiencies and a durable strategy to secure shareholder returns.

Q2 Earnings Show Continued Operating Strengths

In Q2 2025, in New Taiwan dollars, the company delivered record revenue from 38.6% year-over-year growth, predominantly driven by AI and high-performance computing chips. Net income grew by 60.7% year-over-year, with earnings per share up by the same amount. Even stronger growth was exhibited as the company demonstrated strong internal efficiency with a 61.7% year-over-year growth in operating income. Operating expenses were just 9.1% of revenue, down from 10.5% year-over-year, leading to a high operating margin of 49.6%. We can expect further margin strength from the company in 2026, as it is considering price hikes for advanced nodes as there is currently tight capacity and high customer dependency; these are truly effects of a world-class moat.

It's not surprising that the company is experiencing so much growth as the AI and high-performance computing opportunity is now in full bloom. High-performance computing (which includes AI semiconductors) is the dominant revenue share, at about 60%, up from 52% a year ago. Smartphones are approximately 27%, down from 33% a year ago. These two segments are the lion's share of the business' cash flows.

North American revenue is a stark 75% of TSMC's total, compared to just 9% for China, which is down sharply year-over-year. This shows, structurally, a major competitive advantage that America and the free world have over China right now. It will be very difficult for China to compete with TSMC using domestic companies, and TSMC is evidently adhering politically to the liberal West. This alliance of TSMC and Western countries (dominantly America) does create a strong level of growth security, as there is lower risk of containment than if the company's revenues were oriented more toward China. That said, with easing China-U.S. relations appearing likely to continue in the medium term, we could see TSMC's Chinese revenue exposure increase again as the country "opens up."

Management provided guidance that was certainly robust. Revenue growth was forecast at around 30% year-over-year, up from ~25%, due to continued expansion of the AI opportunity. Q3 guidance pointed to 38% year-over-year revenue growth. For investors considering buying TSMC stock as a long-term investment, or for those who already own it as such, it's key to notice the evolving structural tailwinds currently fueling sentiment. Management sees AI-related revenues doubling in 2025 and then continuing at a 40-45% compound annual growth rate over the next five years. This AI-related growth trajectory provides significant resilience to short-term consumer electronics softness.

Also, despite very high capital expenditures at present to support infrastructure for burgeoning advanced semiconductor manufacturing demand, the company is still able to deliver substantial free cash flow. Moreover, as the company's moat becomes even more expansive, it is likely that its free cash flow margin will increase moderately further (albeit in cycles), as it will be able to spend less on infrastructure and still increase its prices while maintaining market leadership.

Value Investors Know Now Is Not The Best Time To Buy TSMC Stock

I consider TSMC stock to be much better suited to growth and momentum investors right now, as even though the company still looks "cheap" on paper, based on the reality of market sentiment, it's slightly overextended in price for reliable medium-term alpha to accrue.

The stock is trading well above the 50-week moving average in price, and the 14-week RSI has now essentially breached 70, indicating it is overbought based on a medium-term horizon.

Also, looking at the stock's historical sentiment related to the Wall Street consensus, there's little indication that the stock will reliably outperform the consensus outlook, as it never really has done in the last five years. Indeed, the professional market is, understandably, bullish on TSMC stock. But when a whole market is bullish on a company, it's difficult for it to outperform expectations. Given the reality of hedge funds, retail investors, and most capital allocators using the Wall Street consensus as a reference point for forward sentiment, I can't see TSMC stock delivering outsized near-term alpha. There is, in simple words, a sentiment anchor to this stock that is hard for management to overcome. This is compounded by the maturity of the AI opportunity, likely contracting valuation multiples moderately (rather than expanding them) as top-line growth slows.

My current 12-month price target for TSMC stock is constituted as follows: The current non-GAAP P/E ratio is 30, and given substantially moderating growth in Fiscal 2026 compared to Fiscal 2025, I forecast that this will contract to about 25 in 12 months in a base case. I also forecast about $10.50 in normalized earnings per share for the middle of 2026, indicating a stock price of about $260 in 12 months. The current price is $245, so the upside is marginal, and my rating is Hold as a result.

Certain Operational Risks Also Constrain Sentiment For Now

Currently, TSMC's Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate ("CoWoS") packaging capacity is fully tapped, creating a supply chokepoint. Although the company is rapidly ramping packaging, delays in the company's expansions in Taiwan and Arizona could constrain revenues.

Additionally, broad Trump-era tariffs across steel, aluminum, and (potentially) semiconductors of up to 50% will almost certainly contribute to margin pressures for TSMC. However, because these tariff-related profitability pressures will be affecting almost all companies, sentiment could stay more sustainable than if they were company-specific.

I think the Taiwan geopolitical risk is manageable right now if the Trump administration treats China with respect and diplomatic competition rather than heightening a trade war. Understandably, the U.S. administration wants to create global security after the devastating wars in Ukraine, Israel, Gaza, and Iran. However, deterrence is different from provocation, and Trump's actions now could have long-lasting implications on the markets, particularly on TSMC (as it is central to geopolitical agendas).

Conclusion: Hold

TSMC stock is undoubtedly one of the investments on the market that connects strongly with investors looking for a resilient moat and monopoly-like free cash flow generation. Q2 results were impressive, and there is every indication that sentiment will remain steady for the stock in the near term, but it is unlikely to expand rapidly and deliver ultra-high alpha.

Put simply, TSM stock is most likely moderately overvalued, given the moderation in top-line growth coming in the following financial years. I reiterate my Hold rating, despite such strong Q2 results.

data.title: Fifth District Bancorp: Deep Value At 0.56x Book With Excess Capital
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Adam Peithman as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Fifth District Bancorp (NASDAQ:FDSB) is a recently converted thrift based in New Orleans, Louisiana. They have approximately $531 million in total assets, which makes it very small for a bank. It’s also very traditional, and 90% of its loans are fixed-rate, single-family residential mortgages.

Main Thesis

After its mutual-to-stock conversion, FDSB transitioned from being owned by depositors to being a publicly traded company. The IPO closed on July 31st, 2024, and under the conversion rules, the company can begin buybacks and/or dividends on August 1st, 2025.

While the decision, at the end of the day, lies ultimately with the board, this eligibility milestone is important because it opens the door to shareholder capital returns, should the board pursue them.

Given the bank's overcapitalized balance sheet, the bank's attractive valuation, and historical patterns among its peers, I believe FDSB offers a compelling risk-reward opportunity.

A Look at 2023 Thrift Conversions

I have reviewed every single thrift that converted in 2023, and 100% of them initiated buybacks shortly after their one-year blackout periods expired.

For example, SR Bancorp (SRBK) announced their 10% repurchase program exactly one year after their IPO date, as can be seen in the table above. Similarly, BV Financial (BVFL) announced their 10% buyback program almost one year to the date. The latest announcement came from Mercer Bancorp (OTCQB:MSBB), who announced their share repurchase program 5 months after the end of the blackout period.

Most thrift conversions in 2023, however, (6 out of 8) launched share repurchase programs within 1 to 2 months of their one-year IPO anniversaries, and all of these authorized repurchases of 5–10% of their outstanding shares. Later in the same year, many of these companies, such as NB Bancorp (NBBK) and First Seacoast Bancorp (FSEA), added dividends or additional authorization for buybacks.

Many of these banks, just like FDSB, were trading at deep discounts to TBV, in some cases as low as 0.4x. In my opinion, it’s in these cases that buybacks made the most sense to return capital to shareholders.

FDSB currently trades at just 0.56x P/TBV, which places it squarely in line with several peers who have bought back shares in the last year, and in fact at the lower end.

Furthermore, most of these banks had total risk-based capital ratios of 14-25% just before they began repurchasing stock. By contrast, FDSB sits at 42.5%, which puts it in an even better position to return capital to shareholders.

Balance Sheet & Capital Position

In other words, FDSB is sitting on tons of capital, far more than it needs.

As we mentioned already, as of FDSB's March 31, 2025 call report (see page 53), they had a total risk-based capital ratio of 42.5%. Regulators only need 10% for a bank to be considered “well-capitalized.” In other words, FDSB has over four times more capital than it needs, and very few places to put it to work.

The banking landscape is extremely competitive, especially for such a small company. And since FDSB is run conservatively with assets that are mostly low-yielding mortgages, it makes the most sense, in my opinion, to return capital to shareholders via buybacks and/or dividends, especially when the stock trades at around half its book value.

It fits the classic Buffett scenario: buying a dollar for 50 cents.

Financial Snapshot

Earnings at FDSB have been flat the last couple of years because of rising interest rates, and they earned only $78,000 in net income in Q1 2025. However, their net interest margin (NIM) increased in Q1 2025 to 2.41%, compared to just 1.81% in the same quarter last year. This could improve further if the Fed continues to lower rates, as is expected, since deposit costs tend to fall faster than loan yields.

Still, this isn’t a bank that’s going to grow earnings meaningfully, even if interest rates drop. The real value lies in the balance sheet and returning capital to shareholders through buybacks and/or dividends, even if earnings stay flat.

Valuation

At the current market price (CMP) of $12.88, FDSB trades at just 0.56x tangible book value (TBV/PS of $22.89). This is extremely cheap by any bank standard, especially for one with a strong balance sheet and no credit issues.

If the company initiates buybacks, for example, and book value per share increases as a result, then even assuming the market continues to apply the same 0.56x multiple, the share price would rise significantly. The market is pricing FDSB as if it’s a troubled bank, when in reality it’s sitting on cash.

How Much Might They Buy Back?

While buybacks are not guaranteed, we can model the potential impact on the stock price, if such a share repurchasing plan were approved by the board.

Here are a few scenarios I think could happen (for simplicity’s sake, we will assume the buybacks happen at $12.50):

Bear Case | $2M Buyback

FDSB buys $2 million (160,000 shares) at $12.50, retiring ~2.8% of its 5.56 million shares outstanding.

TBV/PS sits at $22.89. At $12.50, the market values the bank at 0.55x book.

The buyback would increase TBV/PS to $23.19; the implied price (0.55x) would be $12.75.

Upside from $12.50: ~2%.

Base Case | $15M Buyback

FDSB buys $15 million (1.2 million shares), retiring ~21% of shares.

TBV/PS increases to $25.75 → implied price at 0.55x: $14.16.

Upside: ~13%.

Bull Case | $25M Buyback

FDSB buys $25 million (2 million shares), retiring ~36% of shares.

TBV/PS increases to $28.71 → implied price at 0.55x: $15.79.

Upside: ~26.3%.

Fair Value Estimate

While the market currently values FDSB at around 0.56x TBV, I believe this is overly conservative given the bank’s squeaky-clean balance sheet, excess capital, and potential for significant capital returns.

Even applying a multiple of 0.75x–0.85x (which many of their peers trade at) to the current tangible book value of $22.89, we get a fair value range of $17.17 to $19.45 per share, which is still conservative in my view. If buybacks are instituted, TBV/PS will rise, and this range could move even higher.

Leadership Changes & Risks

Until recently, the bank was run by CEO Brian North, who unfortunately passed away a few weeks ago. He invested $500,000 of his own money into the IPO, the maximum amount.

The new interim CEO, Amie Lyons, invested $200,000. Though this is less, it’s still enough, in my opinion, for her and the rest of the board (who are also heavily invested) to be aligned with shareholders.

In my opinion, the main risks for this idea include:

  • Interest Rate Changes: If the Fed increases rates, then margins may become thinner, which would result in FDSB’s small earnings coming under even more pressure.

  • Uncertain Capital Return: Buybacks are not guaranteed, and depend ultimately on board decisions.

  • Unpredictable CEO: We don’t yet know if the new CEO will be shareholder-friendly, but I think this is still likely, given that the board and others have invested a lot into the IPO already.

  • Illiquidity and Volatility: Due to FDSB’s small market cap and low trading volume, there isn’t a lot of liquidity and may be large bid-ask spreads that cause volatility.

The Bottom Line

Overall, FDSB trades at a huge discount to its tangible book value, has no credit issues, and is sitting on over four times the capital it needs to be considered “well-capitalized.” Though the potential for future earnings is not bright, I think such a huge discount to tangible book value is hard to ignore when considering what FDSB could do with its capital. If it turns out that buybacks/dividends are not authorized, there’s still plenty of opportunity for the stock to re-rate, increase its earnings as the Fed decreases rates, and three years out from the IPO date, FDSB could even become an attractive acquisition candidate.

data.title: Kubota: Evaluating Conservative 2025-2028, I Say 'Buy'
data.description:
If there's one sector I've always found interesting for investment, it's tools and machinery for farming and various commercial applications. I'm talking tractors, backhoes, I'm talking dump trucks, I'm talking everything from Caterpillar (CAT), to much, much smaller machines from manufacturers like Jungheinrich (OTCPK:JGHHY) and KION (OTCPK:KIGRY). Over the years, I have invested in fact in all of these companies.

One company that I haven't invested in is Kubota Corporation (KUBTY, 6326:TYO), and that's what we'll be taking a look at today. I haven't covered Kubota before, so I'll deconstruct the company in terms of fundamentals, earnings, leverage, and what else might be relevant for forecasts and valuation before giving a price target.

Kubota Corporation is natively listed in Tokyo under ticker 6326, and native Japanese investing is tricky even for me. My broker recently opened up nations like Switzerland again for digital trading, which has made it a lot easier to push into more or less all continental European markets. However, Asian markets, as well as Australia still, unfortunately, require calling the brokerage for significantly higher fees and complications. This is why I don't yet push for many of these investments.

However, Kubota may be a worthy exception to this rule, just like some other companies are exceptions as well.

Let's see why exactly that is.

Kubota - Japanese farming machinery with a good yield

I've invested in Japanese automotive manufacturers primarily before this, but of course, I've heard of Kubota before. It has a 130+ year history, is headquartered in Osaka, and despite its focus on agricultural and commercial machinery, it works in a number of sectors with a variety of end products. These include things like construction equipment, engines, pipes,, valves, cast metal, and even things like Vending machines and equipment for water purification. That last thing especially caught my eye - if you follow my work, you know I love companies that work in the water sector or with water-related end products.

In terms of drivetrains/engines, the company works both with diesel and gasoline engines, and has experience manufacturing tiny sub-0.27-liter engines, such as the D722 used in mini excavators and the like, to huge ones, such as the 6.1-liter diesel. It works with both air-cooled and liquid-cooled engines. As of late, the company has also added electric drivetrains to its lineup, such as the Kubota LXe EV tractor. What I personally often see in my home geography are the mini excavators, which more often than not are Kubota's design/brand.

Kubota is a 1.86T yen market cap company with a long-term leverage/debt ratio of less than 29.5%. This goes in the vein of most Japanese companies and how these seem to view the concept of debt/leverage, meaning that most of them are extremely low, some under 5%. This is also reflected by the A-rated credit from S&P Global.

The company has a relatively typical volatility for a construction/machinery company exposed to a volatile macro. It misses targets about 25-40% of the time, beats them about 9-27% of the time, and hits them about 20-70% of the time. Varies over time, but overall, Kubota has done well. Unfortunately, long-term trends are less than impressive. Kubota has underperformed, with long-term returns at 20 years below 6.5% and 15 years below 8%. In the short term, the return trends are truly bad, with a 27% 1-year drop next to a 13.5%+ rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) in the meantime. If you've invested in Kubota recently, chances are you've done poorly - but if you, like me, are waiting for valuation opportunities, that may come knocking.

The 1st quarter results for Q1 '25 are out, and they unfortunately cement the negative earnings trend the company currently seems to be in. The top line declined by about 8.1%, with operating margins going down nearly 5% - that's not the amount, I'm talking about a move from a 13.3% margin to 8.6% - same thing for EBIT. Machinery revenue was down about 10%, with the demand level remaining flat in Japan due to a decline in the number of farmers (though improvement is expected due to rice prices). And while there seems to be no indication of demand decline in the NA machinery market for housing, the agricultural sector is seeing signs of a slowdown. Investment sentiment for new machinery continues to be low, even if part of the market has shown indications of a recovery.

Also, climate factors continue to affect the company's sales and profits - this was seen especially in Thailand.

As mentioned, the company has a water segment - unfortunately, the water segment is only one-sixth the size of the machinery segment - and this segment is, as I expected it to be, growing. Growth is coming from a shift to alternative pipes for both new construction and replacement, with both native and overseas demand relatively stable.

Looking at the earnings bridge, the primary impacts that are decreasing Kubota's potential are lower sales, somewhat made up for by higher sales prices, R&D expenses, while FX, and some incentives continue to pressure things as well. All in all, it's primarily lower sales that are responsible for the company dropping from over Y100B in profit to close to Y60B, with a cash flow that, unfortunately, in Q1 '25 turned negative.

Kubota's main correlations are to housing, machinery, and agriculture, with their main markets in Asia in NA. Historically speaking, I would say that the company has done a far more stable performance than most European and NA manufacturers. In terms of adjusted earnings, Kubota has never really gone negative. The company saw a significant pressure period during the GFC, with over 4 years of consecutive EPS losses, but recovered to where you could have made easy, triple-digit returns if you had invested at the time.

That is how you make profitable returns from a company like this. The trailing P/E multiple of this company lies in the single digits. Over time, the company has been at single-digit P/E multiples only a few times, usually trading at 13-17x P/E.

It's currently trading at less than 10x, which is why I'm giving you my introductory article for Kubota - I view this as a potential opportunity for investment, all other things and the potential for the business "being equal," so to speak.

Current estimates for Kubota spell out a worsening market environment for 2025, with little recovery possible in 2026 and 2027. An acceleration in revenue seems more likely between 2027-2029, when markets could recover.

The earnings/EBIT are likely to remain pressured for around 2 years, and won't reach 2023-2024E levels according to forecasts, until 2028. That's 2-3 years of relatively poor returns and earnings, and if the valuation reflects this, then chances are that we may only be at the beginning of the company's declining trend. GAAP EPS forecasts have the company not recover to 2024E levels even in 2028; we'd have to wait until 2029.

Unlike other Japanese businesses, Kubota is, however, expected to retain its level of dividend at about the Y50 level per share or thereabout, with a growing trend all the way until 2029E. The argument for investing in Kubota is ignoring a current, and near-term complex market trend, and investing for a recovery in the future.

The reason I have not done so before is that KION and other companies were much more attractive in terms of price. But over the past 2 years, Kubota has declined from Y2,500 to less than Y1,600 where is today, which is a significant decline of 31.39% even with dividends for the company included.

This marks something we should at the very least look at and evaluate.

Kubota: Valuation for a longer-term upside shows double-digit, 15%+ annualized growth potential

As with most of the companies I cover, appeal comes down to a mix of earnings forecasts and peers, as well as accuracy ratios and geographical considerations. Kubota is a qualitative A-rated company with a well-safeguarded yield of 3.14%. The company has declined significantly in the short term and medium term, which has left it trading at a 9.5x P/E, which over a 10-20 year average implies a significant discount to an average of 16.26x.

The near-term growth rate for the company is challenged. Current estimates are a 28% EPS drop in 2025E, followed by growth rates that in the end come to an average growth of negative 1.34% per year until 2027E. Given the current trends in global agriculture machinery and investment sentiment, I view this as a reasonable assumption and forecast. That means that the company can't really be worth 16x P/E at this time, unless we expect a full recovery to growth. Forecasting at a 14x 5-year average P/E, the upside for the lower 2027E results comes to around 18.6% per year, inclusive of dividends.

Not bad, and 14x P/E is in line with the company's lower average as well. Furthermore, it's cheaper than its peers, which we'll get into in a second. A full recovery to 16x P/e would spell out over 20% annualized RoR. Even working with a 10-11x P/E for the short term, a significant discount would not result in a capital loss. We're still talking about an upside of almost 9.5% per year. So your downside is, I believe, well-protected here based even on an earnings decline over the next few years.

In terms of peers, the second component in my approach, I look at Caterpillar, Deere (D), Cummins (CMI), and throwing in Atlas Copco (OTCPK:ATLKY) for good measure. Every single one of these trades is markedly and significantly higher than Kubota - Caterpillar at more than twice the P/E. Even somewhat impacted Cummins is at 16-17x P/E here, and Kubota has significantly better yield than all of the companies I mention here. It has a somewhat less ideal exposure - agriculture and Asia specifically, but it also has advantages - the water segment, which I believe to be clearly growing.

All in all, I therefore consider Kubota not only to be buyable, but the most appealing of its peer group - and I am not the only one to do so. Average analyst upsides from S&P Global analysts spell out a target range starting at Y1,500, up to Y2,800, with an 11-analyst average of Y1,900. I would put the company at a fair value of P/E 14x, impacted about 10% from its 20-year average and lower than its peers, which comes to a fair value of Y2,270, which gives me a PT of around Y1,800 for a solid upside. Given that the native ticker here is at sub-Y1,600 at the time of publishing, I see a comfortable upside for investing, and rate the company a solid Buy.

The ADR for the company is KUBTY, and KUBTY comes to a $60.5/share PT, given that it's an ORD x5 ADR, meaning one share of KUBTY equals 5 native shares.

Risks and considerations for my positive thesis are as follows.

Risks to Kubota

Kubota is heavily correlated to agricultural macro and trends in Asia, though there's not a company in this peer group that does not have a correlation to a volatile macro. Caterpillar would probably be the least impacted. Beyond heavy correlation to a volatile macro, and your typical cost risks, meaning costs for salaries, R&D, and the like, I don't see a whole lot of risks, and Kubota is large enough where the business is competitive on a global scale. That being said, I believe the primary risk to the company is valuation-related. I do not believe that, especially in the current macro, you want to buy it above Y1,800, which gives it a very narrow corridor of appeal, less than Y300 at this time.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

  • Kubota is a fundamentally solid machinery and industrial company out of Japan. The recent 2 years have seen the company decline nearly 40% due to challenges in terms of growth, and the near term does not see these growth challenges abate until at the very earliest 2027E.
  • Because of this, the company is pressured. However, I view the company's valuation and fundamentals to be very appealing at this stage, and I would go ahead and "Buy" the business at a cheap valuation. Based on earnings estimates and peers, I would say that a 13-14x P/E is a solid fair value estimate, even looking at the declines come to Y2,270 for the native, implying a price target of Y1,800, where a 15% annualized upside is realistic.
  • I, therefore, rate Kubota a "Buy" with a Y1,800 native price target, and may buy shares of the company in the near term.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of Y1,800/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Buy rating as of July 2025.

data.title: AWS Puts Agent-Focused Platform Center Stage
data.description:
AI-powered agents have unquestionably become the hot topic for all the largest tech vendors, as Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN, TSX:AMZN:CA) latest event in New York City once again demonstrated. However, as awareness of the complexities of creating and widely deploying agents has grown, so too has the number of questions regarding how to make that happen.

At AWS Summit, Amazon’s cloud computing division addressed those challenges head-on with a range of offerings covering development and deployment tools for agents, as well as a new marketplace from which customers can purchase finished agent products. In sum, it represented a comprehensive vision that AWS hopes will position it as a compelling platform for companies that want to start integrating agents into their environments, regardless of their level of IT sophistication.

One of the key offerings AWS unveiled was Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, which provides a suite of services for building custom agents that can leverage a wide range of different models and agent-building platforms, including open-source options such as Langchain, CrewAI and LlamaIndex. On top of that, AWS introduced its own new open-source agent-building tool it calls Strands Agents. As AWS VP Swami Sivasubramanian mentioned in his keynote, agents aren’t just the hot new AI buzzword, they represent a fundamental rethinking and evolution of how software applications are built, distributed, and used. That, in turn, makes tools like Strands Agents very important for the future of software and the platforms on which they run.

The key elements available through Bedrock AgentCore include a run-time environment for agents that integrates a range of security, reliability, and scaling capabilities. One of the big questions and concerns regarding agents is the potential impact—both positive and negative—that they can quickly have in a given environment because of their autonomous capabilities. To help build trust around agents, Amazon has integrated a series of guardrails, and it leverages its 10+-year old Lambda serverless architecture in which agents can be run. In addition, the company has integrated identity verification capabilities via AgentCore Identity, including the ability to tap into popular commercial and open-source authentication platforms such as OAuth. On top of that, one of the AgentCore services is an observability tool called AgentCore Observability that allows organizations to track how agents are performing, to see the type of data they’re accessing, and more.

Speaking of data, one of the key requirements for optimizing agents’ performance is to provide access to the specific data sets that need to be leveraged to perform certain workflows. AgentCore Gateway provides tools to access various APIs and other tools to make these connections possible. In addition, Amazon is supporting both the MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) standards in a significant way via Gateway, giving companies more flexibility in the range of data sources, applications, and services to which they can connect.

Another important expectation for agents is the ability to build upon previous interactions through access to both short-term and long-term memory. The AgentCore Memory services makes the process of creating these types of often complicated memory structures much easier, allowing developers to create more compelling (and ultimately more successful) agentic experiences. Finally, for companies to monitor how the specific code of a given agent is processing data, Amazon has also created a service it’s calling AgentCore Code Interpreter that can be used to visualize more complex data interactions with certain types of agents.

In addition to the agent-building capabilities, AWS also announced several other key tools to help with the creation and fine tuning of AI models that can be used to drive these agentic applications. For the company’s family of Nova foundation models, Amazon announced the release of Nova Act, which can be used to drive autonomous actions within browsers. Given how critical browser-based applications and searches are to so many workflows, these capabilities will likely be used extensively for agentic AI applications. In fact, the AgentCore Browser service provides this specific capability within the AgentCore platform. Plus, because AgentCore supports the ability to use multiple models for different parts of an agentic workflow, organizations can leverage Nova Act for certain actions while using other LLMs for reasoning-based actions, for example.

For companies working to train or fine-tune their models, there were two important announcements at Summit. First, the company unveiled new capabilities for its SageMaker AI model-building tool that allows organizations to more easily customize Nova models by linking to their own data sources. Second, the company revealed a new type of S3 storage service called Amazon S3 Vectors that is specifically optimized for cost-effectively storing large amounts of the vector data that’s typically used for model training and fine tuning. Together, these two offerings should make it easier for enterprises to build more customized and effective models. It’s also worth noting that these capabilities can be used independently of any agentic AI work but also fit nicely into the overall custom agent development process.

For companies that would prefer to buy instead of build when it comes to agents, AWS also launched a new marketplace for commercially available agents. At launch, the marketplace already includes over 800 options ranging from general-purpose tools to industry and workflow specific options, highlighting how quickly this market is evolving. To put that in perspective, in conversations with an AWS Marketplace exec, he mentioned that the company’s SaaS-based Marketplace launched with only about 50 partners. The goal with this new Agent Marketplace is to provide a storefront for software partners as well as enable enterprises to easily find solutions to fit their specific needs.

Taken together, the announcements from AWS Summit reflect a very focused effort by the company to position itself at the cutting edge of the hottest new technologies. As with many big tech announcements, much of the news comes before many big real-world deployments of agentic AI have started to happen. But, as companies start to experiment with these technologies, it’s essential to provide a set of tools with which they can begin these efforts. Plus, by creating the Agent Marketplace, AWS is also giving companies an easy way to dip their toes into the agentic world. Similar to GenAI overall, we’re still very early in the agentic AI era, but as these latest announcements confirm, the pace of development continues to be blistering.

data.title: DWX- Is It Better Than VYMI?
data.description:
A Change In Script For DWX

The SPDR S&P International Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:DWX) is a 17-year old product (it was set-up in February 2008). While it has been a long-standing source of price destruction (down by -43% since its inception), it appears to have regained its mojo in 2025.

To elaborate, during the first six-and-a-half months of 2025 alone, DWX has surged by almost 20%, which is almost twice the returns of global stocks, and roughly three times the returns of US stocks.

If DWX’s strong alpha this year has sufficiently piqued your interest in this product, and you’re looking to better understand this product and its chief competitor, this article is for you.

What Does DWX ETF Do?

DWX is a passively managed ETF, which seeks to cover the S&P International Dividend Opportunities Index; the latter essentially covers 100 high-yielding stocks from ~23 countries across the globe (excluding the US).

DWX covers its tracking index by way of sampling, which means it won’t necessarily purchase all the stocks in the index and in the same proportion, but rather just a subset of stocks that have a similar risk and return profile to the index. Typically, ETFs which resort to sampling (rather than replication) tend to produce high tracking errors of double-digit percentages, but that is not the case with DWX, although over the past year, the degree of tracking error has ramped up beyond what most ETFs see.

It’s fair to say that DWX is more of a non-US developed market ETF as it ensures that emerging market [EM] exposure is capped at just 15% of the total portfolio, while it also does not consider any China A shares. EM exposure of DWX is currently also largely coming from the likes of Taiwan and South Korea, both of which are not widely defined as EMs.

How Is DWX’s Tracking Index Built?

The S&P International Dividend Opportunities Index looks for potential high yielding stocks from the S&P Global ex-U.S. BMI Index.

In the first step, China A shares are weeded out. Micro-caps also won’t be considered, as they maintain a minimum market-cap threshold of $500M. Also, stocks with daily traded value of less than $5M won’t be considered.

The next stage is when one gets to ascertain the quality of the high-yielders as quite a few supplementary screeners are put to good use. Firstly, we appreciate that DWX doesn’t pursue businesses that pay out dividends without generating any profits (there’s a requirement of positive reported EPS over the last twelve months). Then these businesses also need to generate ‘Funds from operations’ over ‘dividends’ of 1x (a measure of dividend coverage), dividend growth over three years (although declines are permitted so long as it is less than 5%). And finally, these stocks also need to have a dividend yield that is higher than the median dividend yield of the remaining universe (by remaining universe we mean stocks from the S&P Global ex-U.S. BMI Index universe, which have also cleared the $500m market-cap and $5m daily trading barrier).

All in all, we appreciate that DWX has not blindly just picked up the highest yielding 100 stocks from across the world, but has also put in place these investability and sustainability screeners.

In the next stage, DWX then attempts to pick stocks on the basis of a risk-adjusted yield metric, which helps focus on high yielders that don’t witness a great deal of variability in their yields. To be more specific, the dividend yield of a certain stock is divided by the 36-month volatility of the monthly dividend yield. Eventually the chosen stocks are weighted on the basis of the trailing twelve-month dividend yield.

Note that the index gets rebalanced twice a year, and it also takes steps to cap sector and country weights at 25%, and single stock weight at 3% (also as mentioned in the previous section of this article, there is an EM cap of 15%).

Is DWX Better Than VYMI?

Those who’d like to pursue high-yielding international equities via the passive ETF route, are typically directed towards the Vanguard International High Dividend Yield ETF (VYMI) which tracks the FTSE All-World ex US High Dividend Yield Index. VYMI’s popularity can be gleaned by its gargantuan AUM of close to $12B (whereas DWX’s AUM is far lower at less than $0.5B), which it has managed, despite only being around for less than 10 years. Since VYMI attracts most of the ETF flows within the international high yielding space, it would make more sense to measure DWX against this juggernaut, rather than reviewing DWX in isolation.

Unlike DWX, VYMI’s tracking index doesn’t impose too many screeners in the selection process. It essentially looks for its stocks from the FTSE All-World Index which covers both domestic and EM candidates, although REITs are not considered. VYMI doesn’t seek to maintain any restrictions on EMs unlike DWX (VYMI’s current EM exposure is at 22%). While VYM doesn’t really incorporate any clear dividend sustainability screeners, it does not consider stocks that are forecasted to pay zero dividends over the next 12 months. Unlike DWX’s tracking index which follows quite a sophisticated methodology, the goal here is basically to just pick the top 50% of high yielders (based on the forward yield) from the FTSE All-World Index. As a result, note that VYMI is also much larger in scope, covering over 1650 stocks (whereas DWX just covers 100 stocks). DWX also tilts more towards mid-caps, whereas VYMI is more of a giant-cap play. So what are the end results?

Well, as you can see from the image below, both on a price basis, as well as a total return basis (which incorporates dividends), it is VYMI which comes out on top.

Having said that, prima facie, it looks like DWX has the lower risk profile of the two, with a rolling volatility profile that is 140bps lower.

But does that really leave an inimical mark when it comes to the risk-adjusted return track record? Well for that, one may consider looking at the Sharpe ratio (it measures the excess returns of an ETF over the risk-free rate, relative to the standard deviation of the ETF) of these two products across different periods. Be it over the past five years, three years or one year, DWX has consistently lagged its larger peer on the Sharpe ratio front.

VYMI also comes across as the preferable bet for value conscious investors, as it is priced at a P/E discount of -27% to DWX, yet also offers slightly better long-term earnings growth potential.

Even if one wants to gauge the structural qualities of both these products, we feel VYMI is the better product. Firstly, it is more cost accessible, coming in at an expense ratio of just 0.17%, while DWX is 2.65x higher. Because DWX takes great efforts to maintain various caps, it also resorts to a great deal of churn (6 out of 10 stocks get turned over every year) whereas VYMI is a lot more stable, witnessing annual churn of a little over 11%. Then, short-term market players who place a lot of emphasis on getting good fills won’t be troubled by pursuing VYMI, given its relatively tight spreads of 0.04% (DWX’s spreads are 3x more). Finally also note that while both products distribute quarterly, VYMI has grown its dividends at a faster pace, and currently offers a better yield than DWX.

Closing Thoughts

As implied in the previous section, it is rather clear that DWX comes across as an inferior ETF for those looking for exposure to high-yielding international equities. We would prefer to play this theme via VYMI.

data.title: LQD: Slim Spreads And Rising Risks
data.description:
The margin of safety is always dependent on the price paid. It will be large at one price, small at some higher price, nonexistent at some still higher price.

- Benjamin Graham

Investment-grade bonds, just like equities, require an adequate margin of safety to truly protect investors in uncertain times. At the moment, we do not see much, if any, margin of safety with investment grade corporate bonds, for which we can use a popular ETF such as the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:LQD) as a proxy.

As Benjamin Graham's student, Warren Buffett once said, "the three most important words in investing are margin of safety". For corporate bonds that margin of safety comes in the form of real yield, and spread over Treasuries with similar maturities. This interest rate spread is necessary to compensate for potential defaults and the additional risk investors are taking by lending to more risky entities. Corporate bonds also have a higher correlation to the stock market, making them less useful for diversification purposes, another reason they should provide more yield.

There are already signs of potential trouble ahead, commercial bank loan delinquencies have been trending higher for several quarters, several companies now have to refinance cheap bonds issued during the near-zero interest rate environment at much higher rates, and recently some high-profile companies have been downgraded by credit agencies. To give some examples, Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) was downgraded by Moody's (MCO) last June as the company announced plans to split its business, with its senior unsecured debt now at Ba1 making them junk bonds. Another example is Ford Motor Company (F), which is at risk of having its credit cut to junk after S&P Global (SPGI) affirmed the company's 'BBB-' rating, but revised the outlook to negative in February as a result of weaker-than-expected profitability and tariffs risks. This makes Ford Motor a potential fallen angel, and one of the largest by the amount of debt. Both these companies are currently found in LQD's holdings.

Portfolio Characteristics

There are a few things to like about LQD, including a relatively low expense ratio of 0.14%, significant diversification with almost three thousand holdings, and positive and relatively high convexity compared to a bond ETF like the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG). All else equal, positive convexity is better as it means larger price increases when yields fall compared to price declines when yields increase. The reason ETFs like AGG have lower convexity is that they tend to include callable bonds and mortgage-backed securities (MBS), which often exhibit negative convexity given the borrower's option to call or prepay the bonds. Less appealing is the relatively high effective duration of 7.98 years, which is close to the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), which has an effective duration of 7 years and we will use for comparison.

We are also not impressed with the average credit quality, with roughly 43% of the ETF's value in BBB rated debt. Some of these companies risk becoming fallen angels, no longer being considered investment grade, and are often sold at meaningful discounts after the companies are stripped of their investment-grade credit rating. In general, being a forced seller leads to selling at a discount to fair value. On the other side of this trade are ETFs like the iShares Fallen Angels USD Bond ETF (FALN) and the VanEck Fallen Angel High Yield Bond ETF (ANGL), which have tended to outperform as they purchase at a discount.

Despite the wide diversification, there is a relatively high concentration to the banking sector, with roughly 23% of market value invested in this category, followed by consumer non-cyclical at over 17% of total market value. The banking exposure is particularly concerning, as financial institutions are usually highly leveraged, and one important failure can cascade through the industry, as we can remember from the global financial crisis.

Slim Spreads

Still, these issues might not be that important if the ETF were trading with a significant valuation margin of safety. However, the spread between LQD's dividend yield, and a Treasury Bond ETF with a similar duration, such as IEF, is near the lowest in a decade. Investors are getting very little in terms of additional yield to compensate for the credit risk, as well as the higher correlation that corporate bonds have with equity markets.

Credit Defaults

We already mentioned some recent notable downgrades, but looking at the overall picture it is clear that credit metrics have been deteriorating for some time. For example, the commercial banks delinquency rate on loans has been on an uptrend over the past couple of years, and it is now close to the ten-year average. In their second-quarter of 2025 investment-grade credit check report, S&P Global noted that potential fallen angels outweigh potential rising stars, or companies that could move from junk to investment grade, by over 70%. This is a clear sign that credit quality momentum is in decline.

Tariffs and Recession Risks

While the odds of a recession have recently receded, they remain high by historical standards. Most economists also agree that a worst-case scenario trade war would increase the odds of a recession significantly. Tariffs would certainly impact some industries and companies directly, but others could be affected indirectly as well as a result of the general economic slowdown. At the same time, consumer sentiment is close to a multi-decade low, although it has slightly rebounded recently.

Alternatives Comparison

While past performance is no guarantee of future returns, there are structural reasons why ETFs like FALN and ANGL are in a good position to outperform LQD over the long-term. These ETFs aim to purchase companies that recently lost their investment-grade credit rating, but are usually still far from default. With many investors forced to sell these bonds, their prices tend to be overly punished beyond what the downgrade would justify. The JNK ETF has also outperformed LQD, but we are more concerned about its credit quality and do not see a structural tailwind. In the case of the IEF ETF, total return has been extremely low as the ETF digests the impact of bonds bought when Treasuries were yielding near zero interest. Still, IEF could outperform in a rush to safe havens and has on average a lower correlation to equity markets.

Valuation

The trailing-twelve-months dividend yield is close to the ten-year average, the thirty day SEC yield is 5.2%, and the average yield to maturity is 5.3%. The problem is that Treasury yields have significantly increased, with IEF's dividend yield ~120 bps above its ten-year average. LQD's yield appears reasonable, until it is compared to safer alternatives.

Risks

While high-grade corporate bonds have a reputation as relatively stable investments, they can sometimes hide significant risks, especially when markets become complacent. Inflation remains elevated, interest rates uncertain, and credit risk is rapidly deteriorating, as seen by some notable credit downgrades in recent months. The key risk with LQD, though, is that its interest spread over Treasuries has compressed, which is particularly concerning at a time of economic uncertainty and deteriorating credit risk.

Conclusion

The interest rate spread of high-grade corporate bonds with equivalent maturity Treasury bonds has compressed significantly, reducing the margin of safety in case of defaults and delinquencies. As inflation remains elevated, interest rates uncertain, and credit risks escalate, investors holding bond ETFs like LQD might be unknowingly exposed to greater downside potential than anticipated.

When the yield premium shrinks too low, even wide diversification won’t fully shield investors from the effects of tariff disruptions or an economic downturn. For investors willing to consider higher risk alternatives we believe fallen angel ETFs have a structural advantage, and offer a more attractive yield. Alternatively, for investors that prefer taking less risk, an intermediate Treasury bond ETF like IEF might be worth considering.
